coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of core-shell Ag@CD oxygen reduction catalyst under ultraviolet irradiation using carbon dot as reducing agents
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Min
Chu, Siyu
Ma, Yifan
Wang, Xuzhe
Li, Zijiong
coredata.dc:description: The widespread applications of fuel cell depend on the reasonable designment of excellent catalysts that are easy to synthesize or abundant in reserves for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The application of precious metal catalysts (such as Pt, Pd etc.) is limited due to their scarcity of resources and high cost, while Ag/C catalysts have shown unique advantages in fuel cell and are expected to replace precious metal catalysts. In this paper, the fabrication of core–shell Ag@carbon dot (Ag@CD) with varying Ag loadings was achieved through a one-step method utilising CD as both a reducing agent and stabiliser, with the assistance of ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. Its suitable silver salt concentration and stable core–shell structure are the fundamental reasons for the excellent catalytic performance of ORR. The results show that the limiting current density (5.8 mA cm−2) of Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst is much greater than that of Ag@CD-0.05 and Ag@CD-0.2 catalysts. The Tafel slope of Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst is almost equivalent to those for Pt/C catalysts, suggesting the Ag@CD-0.1 exhibits an outstanding catalytic activity. The relative current density of Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst only decreased by 2.5 % after 50,000 s, suggesting that Ag@CD-0.1 exhibits more excellent stability for ORR, which is ascribing to the carbon layer coated outside the Ag nanoparticles (NPs).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fuel cell
Carbon dot
Catalysts
Ultraviolet irradiation
Oxygen reduction reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic illustration of synthesis of Ag@CD.
TEM images of (a) Ag@CD-0.05, (b) Ag@CD-0.1, (c) Ag@CD-0.2; (d) The TEM image and (the inset image in d) the particle size distribution of CD; (e-f) The HRTEM image of Ag@CD-0.1.

(a-b) XRD patterns of CD, Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, and Ag@CD-0.2, respectively. (c) Raman spectra of CD, Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, and Ag@CD-0.2, respectively. (d) XPS spectra of CD, Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, and Ag@CD-0.2, respectively. (e–f) High-resolution XPS spectra of Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, and Ag@CD-0.2: (e) Ag 3d, (f) N 1s, respectively.

The (a) CV of Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, and Ag@CD-0.2 tested in O2-saturated KOH, respectively; The (b) LSV of Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, Ag@CD-0.2 and 20 % Pt/C respectively; (c) The LSV curves of Ag@CD-0.1 tested in O2-saturated KOH with different scan rates ranging from 400 to 2025 rpm; (d) The onset potential, half-wave potential and current density of Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, Ag@CD-0.2 and 20 % Pt/C respectively.

(a) Tafel plots of Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, Ag@CD-0.2 and 20 % Pt/C respectively; (b) EIS of Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1, and Ag@CD-0.2 tested in O2-saturated KOH, respectively; (c) The corresponding Koutecky-Levich plots (J−1 vs ω−1/2) of Ag@CD-0.1 at 0.725 V, 0.75 V and 0.775 V.

The CV curves of (a) Ag@CD-0.05, (b) Ag@CD-0.1, (c) Ag@CD-0.2 before and after 5000 cycles; (d) The Amperometric i-t curves of Ag@CD-0.1 and 20 % Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at 0.8 V vs RHE potential for 12 h.
The content distribution of different forms of nitrogen in the sample.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of core-shell Ag@CD oxygen reduction catalyst under ultraviolet irradiation using carbon dot as reducing agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Electronics and Information, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou 450002, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Electronics and Information, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou 450002, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Key Laboratory of Magnetoelectronic Information Functional Materials, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou 450002, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Key Laboratory of Magnetoelectronic Information Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Key Laboratory of Magnetoelectronic Information Functional Materials, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou 450002, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: School of Electronics and Information, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry, Zhengzhou 450002, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The widespread applications of fuel cell depend on the reasonable designment of excellent catalysts that are easy to synthesize or abundant in reserves for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). The application of precious metal catalysts (such as Pt, Pd etc.) is limited due to their scarcity of resources and high cost, while Ag/C catalysts have shown unique advantages in fuel cell and are expected to replace precious metal catalysts. In this paper, the fabrication of core–shell Ag@carbon dot (Ag@CD) with varying Ag loadings was achieved through a one-step method utilising CD as both a reducing agent and stabiliser, with the assistance of ultraviolet (UV) irradiation. Its suitable silver salt concentration and stable core–shell structure are the fundamental reasons for the excellent catalytic performance of ORR. The results show that the limiting current density (5.8 mA cm−2) of Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst is much greater than that of Ag@CD-0.05 and Ag@CD-0.2 catalysts. The Tafel slope of Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst is almost equivalent to those for Pt/C catalysts, suggesting the Ag@CD-0.1 exhibits an outstanding catalytic activity. The relative current density of Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst only decreased by 2.5 % after 50,000 s, suggesting that Ag@CD-0.1 exhibits more excellent stability for ORR, which is ascribing to the carbon layer coated outside the Ag nanoparticles (NPs).

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenges posed by energy depletion and environmental pollution can be addressed by vigorously promoting the development of alternative and sustainable energy sources. Among many alternative energy systems, electrochemical energy conversion and storage devices have set off a huge wave of research. Among them, Proton exchange membrane fuel cells have garnered significant interest because they can convert the chemical energy of fuels into electrical energy without additional steps and are characterised by high energy conversion efficiency and zero pollution [1,2]. However, the slow ORR kinetics and high manufacturing costs severely limit the widespread application of fuel batteries [3,4]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to improve the catalytic efficiency and durability of catalysts through new technologies to reduce the dependence on precious metal catalysts such as Pt and Pd [5–8]. One commonly used strategy is to evenly disperse precious metal catalysts on high conductivity and large specific surface area carbon nanomaterials [9,10]. In this way, not only can the effective specific surface area of the electrocatalyst be maximized, but also the mass transfer of reactants to the surface of the electrocatalyst can be more efficient and rapid [11].

Ag NPs have unique physicochemical properties and are widely used in the fields of catalysis, antimicrobial, biomedicine and sensors [12–14]. The preparation of Ag NPs is mainly categorized into physical and chemical methods. The chemical method is sought after by researchers for its advantages of simple operation, reproducibility and mild reaction conditions. However, the chemical reduction of silver salts cannot be achieved without the use of reducing agents such as sodium borocyanide [15], which in turn can have a harsh impact on the environment; the process of removing the excess stabilizers may cause agglomeration of NPs, which is not conducive to the contact between the reactive substances and the active sites of Ag NPs, thus reducing the catalytic performance of Ag NPs. In order to solve the above problems, it is very important to find the unique reducing agent or develop new methods to make the preparation of Ag NPs simpler and better performance.

Carbon nanomaterials, as reported, are widely used in catalysts, catalyst carriers, supercapacitors, etc. because of their large specific surface area, high porosity, simple preparation, and special physical and chemical properties [16,17]. For example, Lv et al.[18] studied the ORR and hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) properties of β-graphyne/graphyne nanoribbons (βGyNRs/βGDyNRs) by density functional theory simulation. The results show that βGyNRs/βGDyNRs are excellent bifunctional carbon-based metal-free catalysts after functionalization by adjusting the edge shape or N doping. In addition, of those carbon materials, Carbon dot (CD) exhibits a combination of the excellent physical and chemical properties of graphene, as well as a series of new properties resulting from oxygen-containing functional groups, surface effects, quantum confinement effects, and edge effects, which are beneficial for the construction of support structures, mass transfer functions, and active centers in composite materials [19,20]. As a result, they are favored in various fields such as chemistry, physics and materials [21]. Indeed, CD functions as an effective electron donor [22], capable of acting as a reducing agent to reduce metal ions and thereby contributing to the formation of metal/CD NPs [23–25]. In recent years, there has been a spate of literature on the preparation of metal NPs as sensors or catalysts using CD [26].

As early as 2012, Luo et al. prepared Au@CD composites with a 2 nm shell layer of CD by reducing HAuCl4 with CD at 100 ℃ [27]. In the following year, Wang et al. found that CD can directly reduce Au NPs from chloroauric acid without any other additives or stabilizers [28]. Liu et al. synthesized Ag NPs in a one-step process using fluorescent CD as reducing and stabilizing agents and investigated their antimicrobial properties. The results showed that such Ag NPs had good stability and excellent bactericidal activity when the CD was doped with sulfur (S) and the concentration of Ag atoms was 150 µM. The bactericidal mechanism of Ag NPs was attributed to the fact that the smaller sized Ag NPs synthesized by S-doped CD could penetrate the E. coli membrane and interact with DNA and respiratory chain [29,30]. It must be added that the claim that ultraviolet irradiation can effectively reduce nano metal particles has already been confirmed in 2012 [31]. Compared with traditional methods of preparing nano metal ions, ultraviolet irradiation reduction method has the advantages of no pollution, low toxicity, no need for reducing agents, simple and safe operation, and has broad application prospects.

In fact, the advantages of Ag/CD are not only reflected in photocatalysis, but also in electrocatalytic potential. The preparation of Ag/CD is a simple one-step process that does not require complex laboratory equipment or difficult post-processing. Herein, we prepared CD with a mean diameter of 4.45 nm by microwave synthesis, and then employed a simple one-step UV irradiation reduction method to obtain core–shell nanomaterials (Ag@CD). The impact of nanosilver concentration on the electrocatalytic ORR performance of the products was mainly investigated, and the results showed that the Ag@CD-0.1 exhibited the most favourable performance. The half-wave potential in a 0.1 M KOH electrolyte solution (0.87 V vs.0.85 V) exhibited superior performance compared to that of the commercial Pt/C, with an electron transfer number approaching 4, indicative of favourable ORR electrocatalytic activity. In addition, after nearly 50,000 s of stability test, only a minor reduction of 2.5 % was observed, which proved the excellent stability of the catalysts.

Citric Acid (CA, 99.5 %), urea (99.0 %) and Silver Nitrate (AgNO3, 99.0 %) are supported by a company named Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd., in China. The ultrapure water used throughout the experiment process was prepared by our own laboratory. In addition, all reagents are analytical grade and do not need further purification.

CA and urea reacted at a certain molar ratio under 750 W microwave conditions for a period of 13 min in order to obtain a CD stock solution. Specifically, a solution was prepared by dissolving 0.84 g of citric acid (CA) and 0.72 g of urea in 15 ml of deionized water, and the solution was then placed in a pre-heated microwave oven for 11 min until the solution changed from colorless to burnt brown, indicating that the reaction had reached completion. Once the reactants had cooled down to ambient temperature, an additional 15 ml of deionized water was incorporated into the mixture, followed by thorough stirring to ensure that any residue at the bottom of the container was fully dissolved. Finally, the CD powder was obtained by vacuum filtration (0.22 μm, Jinlong), dialysis (MD77-1000, JielePu) and freeze-drying.

A total of 3.7 ml of 0.05 M, 0.1 M and 0.2 M AgNO3 solutions were prepared for use, and then 5 ml of CD solution (0.8 mg ml−1) was added to the above solution at a slow rate. Following a 30-minute stirring period, the Ag NPs were obtained through UV irradiation at 365 nm for a period of 2 h. Finally, the excess CD was washed with ethanol and the samples were collected by freeze-drying. The obtained products were named Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1 and Ag@CD-0.2, respectively. The schematic illustration of synthesis of Ag@CD was shown in Fig. 1 .
The sample characterization and electrochemical characterization are presented in the supplementary documents.

CD has been proved to be both a reducing agent for the in-situ growth of Ag NP and a capping agent for covering Ag NP[32,33]. The in-situ reduction method is used to use CD as a template to grasp the reducibility and stability of the CD themselves. Coupled with the auxiliary effect of UV irradiation, a thin layer of carbon shell will be reduced on the surface to form an Ag@CD structure. Ag+ in the solution was reduced to Ag by UV irradiation to produce hydrated electrons and reducing free radical groups in the solution. The method has the characteristics of uniform particles, high dispersion, large particle size and good reproducibility. The morphology and structure of the original CD and the Ag@CD catalysts were studied by TEM (Fig. 2 ). We mainly investigated the effect of AgNO3 concentration on the morphology of the resulting Ag@CD nanostructures. As shown in Fig. 2a-c, the size of Ag@CD particle distributes homogeneously when the concentration of AgNO3 was low (Fig. 2a and c), while as the concentration of AgNO3 increased to 0.2 M, the nanoparticles were agglomerated and the size rapidly increased. Therefore, in our study, we speculated that Ag@CD with a fixed concentration of 0.1 M AgNO3 may be most conducive to the enhancement of electrocatalytic ORR. As can be seen from Fig. 2d, the carbon dots were uniformly dispersed and spherical with particle sizes ranging from 2 nm-6 nm. The well-dispersed core–shell structure Ag@CD-0.1 NPs could also be clearly observed from the TEM images (Fig. 2e-f and Fig. S2), with spherical Ag NPs nuclei (dark color) and carbon shells (light color) with a thickness of about 2 nm. The outermost layer of the CD served as a capping agent to stabilize the Ag particles. Lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.238 nm can be observed from the HRTEM image (Fig. 2e), which were attributed to the (111) crystalline facets of Ag, indicating the formation and good crystallinity of the Ag particles. The Ag (111) lattice plane demonstrated a comparatively weaker affinity for *OH adsorption, which endowed it with superior catalytic efficiency in the oxygen reduction reaction [34]. As a result, the (111) facet of Ag@CD was instrumental in bolstering the overall ORR activity. Meanwhile, the Ag particles encapsulated by a carbon layer serve a dual function: they shield the reactive components from corrosion by the electrolyte, and they facilitate the interaction between the reactants and the active sites on the catalyst [35], which is beneficial for improving the corrosion resistance and stability of the catalyst.

The chemical structure of the prepared catalyst Ag@CD was characterized by XRD and Raman spectra. The structures of Ag@CD catalysts were characterized by XRD, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The peak located around 25° was the graphite (002) crystalline facet of carbon, which was consistent with the XRD peak of CD [25]. Meanwhile, the extremely weak characteristic peak of graphite (0 0 2) (Fig. 3b) and obvious characteristic peak of Ag (Fig. 3a) could also be observed, suggesting the successfully recombination of CD and Ag. The four clear diffraction peaks were detected at 38°, 44°, 64°, and 78°, which correspond to the specific crystal planes of silver that are arranged in a face-centered cubic structure, namely the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), and (3 1 1) planes [36]. In addition, no clear characteristic peaks of silver oxide were observed indicating that the Ag in this catalyst exists mainly in metallic form.

Fig. 3c showed the Raman spectra of CD, Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1 and Ag@CDs-0.2 catalysts. The clear D-band (1371 cm−1) and G-band (1531 cm−1) signals [37] could be captured from the Raman spectrogram of Ag@CD catalysts, which were attributed to the intrinsic peaks of carbon. In addition, some surface functional group information of the catalyst could also be observed from the Raman patterns. The Raman peaks at 618 cm−1 and 779 cm−1 were attributed to the non-out-of-plane deformation vibration of the OC-N bond and the non-asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-N bond, respectively; and the two weak peaks at 1151 cm−1 and 1245 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-O bond [37,38]. The defect peaks and other peaks (such as OC-N, C-N and C-O etc.) facilitated anchoring the Ag nanoparticles, thus improving the catalytic activity and stability toward ORR.

The qualitative analysis of surface elements is achieved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) testing of the binding energy of the primary electrons. Elements C, N and O were detected as present in CD by XPS full spectrograms (Fig. 3d) in Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1 and Ag@CD-0.2 (Tabs. S3-S5). The percentages of Ag in Ag@CD-0.05, Ag@CD-0.1 and Ag@CD-0.2 were 2.36 %, 5.67 % and 8.97 %, respectively. The content of Ag in Ag@CD-0.2 catalyst was the highest, while in the process of cleaning and removing excess AgNO3, it was easy to cause the agglomeration of Ag particles, thereby reducing the active sites exposed outside, which was not conducive to the catalytic process of ORR. The spectral peaks observed at 368 eV and 374 eV are attributed to the energy levels of Ag 3d5/2 and Ag 3d3/2, as depicted in Fig. 3e. The spin energy separation was 6 eV, indicating that Ag was metallic[39], which was attributing to completely reduction of Ag+ to Ag0 by CD serving as reducing agents, thus facilitating the catalytic activity toward ORR.

The N1s XPS spectra were deconvoluted into Oxidized-N, graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N and pyridinic-N, respectively[40] and the element concentration (atomic percent, at.%) were shown in Table S4 and Table 1 . Actually, the presence of nitrogen in CD increases the material defects on one side and changes the electron distribution of carbon atoms on the other side, which improves its oxygen reduction catalytic performance [10,25,41]. It has been reported in the literature that the carbon atoms in close proximity to the pyridinic- N are easily adsorbed and reacted with OH, so that an increase in its concentration is conducive to enhanced the catalytic performance in oxygen reduction[41]. The highest concentration of pyridinic-N and Ag (111) in Ag@CD-0.1 (Fig. 3f) synergistically facilitating to enhancing the catalytic activity of ORR[42]. The C 1s spectral peaks (as depicted in Figs. S1a-c) identified three distinct carbon environments: OC-N/OC-O at 288.8 eV, C–O at 287.8 eV, and C–C/CC at 285.5 eV, which are consistent with the Raman spectroscopy findings. In the O 1 s spectrum (Figs. S1d-f), two prominent peaks were detected, signifying the presence of CO at 531.6 eV and C-O at 532.4 eV within the Ag@CD nanocomposite.

The electrochemical performance of Ag@CD catalysts was evaluated in an oxygen-saturated 0.1 M KOH electrolyte, and it was suggested that Ag@CD-0.1 exhibits more outstanding catalytic performance than that of 20 % Pt/C toward ORR (Fig. 4 ). The ORR electrocatalytic performance of the original CD was discussed in our previous work[25]. The oxygen reduction peak of Ag@CD-0.1 was 0.89 V, which was 10 mV and 30 mV positive that that of Ag@CD-0.05 and Ag@CD-0.2, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4a. The LSV curves showed that the onset potential and half-wave potential Ag@CD-0.05 were 0.97 V and 0.76 V (Tabs. S1-2). As the concentration of Ag increasing, the onset potential and half-wave potential of Ag@CD-0.1 were 1.01 V and 0.87 V, shifted positively by 40 mV and 110 mV respectively (Fig. 4b). When the Ag content continued to increase, the onset potential and half-wave potential were negatively shifted by 20 mV and 80 mV, respectively, compared with Ag@CD-0.2 (Fig. 4b). The current density of Ag@CD-0.1 was 5.8 mA cm−2, which was better than that of 20 % Pt/C (Fig. 4d). From the above analysis, it could be seen that with the increase of Ag content, the catalytic performance showed a trend of first enhancing and then weakening. This was because when the Ag concentration was low, the number of catalytic active sites was less, resulting in poorer catalytic activity for ORR; An increase in Ag concentration will cause an increase in the number of catalytically active sites and the catalytic performance will move towards a more favourable trend; however, when the Ag concentration is increasing, the chances of the silver particles contacting each other per unit volume will be greatly increased. The specific surface area and surface energy of the nanoparticles will be attracted to each other through chemical bonding at higher concentrations, leading to the appearance of agglomerates [43–45]; As the nanoparticles further form larger agglomerates, the available catalytic surface area will be reduced, resulting in a significant loss of catalytic performance [46,47]. In order to elucidate the number of electrons transferred in the catalytic process, the Ag@CD catalysts were subjected to analysis using a rotating disk electrode (RDE) technique. Fig. 4c illustrates the ORR measurements for the Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst at a variety of rotational velocities, spanning from 400 to 2025 rpm, within an oxygen-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution.

To assess the kinetic characteristics of the ORR, the Tafel slopes for various samples were derived from LSV measurements (Fig. 5 a). The Tafel slope of the Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst, determined in a 0.1 M KOH solution, was measured to be 85 mV per decade, which is lower than that of the Ag@CD-0.05 catalyst at 120 mV per decade and the Ag@CD-0.2 catalyst at 122 mV per decade, but slightly higher than the commercial Pt/C catalyst, which exhibited a Tafel slope of 81 mV per decade [48]. This indicated that the Ag@CD-0.1 had a more rapid kinetic rate and superior catalytic performance. The kinetic behavior of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) for the Ag@CD-0.1 electrocatalyst was additionally assessed by correlating the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data with a streamlined equivalent circuit representation (Fig. 5b). It is a well-established understanding that the diameter of the semicircle evident in the low-frequency region is indicative of the resistance to charge transfer; Accordingly, a smaller semicircle indicated a more expeditious charge transfer process. From Fig. 5b, it could be observed that Ag@CD-0.1 the radius was the smallest, implying that the kinetics of the transfer of intermediate ions and electrons are comparatively fast, thus facilitating to improve the catalytic activity toward ORR.

The Koutecky-Levich (K-L) slopes were derived from LSV measurements conducted at various rotation speeds. These slopes enabled us to ascertain the quantity of electrons engaged in the transfer process throughout the oxygen reduction reaction. The K-L diagram showed that the number of electron transfers was around 3.94 at 0.725 V–0.775 V (Fig. 5c), suggesting a nearly 4e− transfer process.

The durability of the synthesized catalysts was evaluated through accelerated degradation testing (ADT). As illustrated in Fig. 6 a-d, the peak potential for Ag@CD exhibits only a minor shift after undergoing 5000 cycles of CV and LSV (Fig. S3), which indicated its remarkable stability. Additionally, the Ag@CD catalyst retained 97.5 % of its current density even after a prolonged period of 50,000 s, contrasting with the commercial Pt/C catalyst, which experienced a 2.5 % decrease over the same timeframe. These findings underscore the superior stability of Ag@CD for oxygen reduction reactions in alkaline environments. This impressive performance was believed to be largely attributed to the carbon layer coated outside the Ag nanoparticles, which not only could prevent the active components from being corroded by the electrolyte but also make the contact between reactants and active substances. Therefore, the Ag@CD catalysts with a core–shell structure exhibit a good durability toward ORR.

In this study, Ag@CD catalyst with core–shell structure was prepared by one-step synthesis process using CD as reducing agent and stabilizing material. After 50,000 s of cycling, the relative current density is only slightly reduced by 2.5 %. The excellent ORR catalytic performance is mainly due to the appropriate silver salt concentration and stable core–shell structure. The results show that the Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst has a higher limiting current density of up to 5.8 mA cm−2, exceeding the values of Ag @ CD-0.05 (5.3 mA/cm2) and Ag @ CD-0.2 (5.5 mA cm−2) catalysts. In addition, the Tafel slope Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst is comparable to the Pt/C catalyst. These observations indicate that the Ag@CD-0.1 catalyst not only outperforms commercial Pt/C in terms of catalytic activity for oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), but also maintains commendable stability.

Min Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation. Siyu Chu: Visualization, Methodology, Data curation. Yifan Ma: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xuzhe Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Zijiong Li: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by The National Natural Science Foundation of China (12474176); Key Research & Development and promotion projects in Henan Province (grant numbers 22104030021, 242102240080).

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
